item.title: Human Rights Commission calls for investigation into school preaching incident
item.description: The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has called on the Ministry of Education to promptly investigate allegations that a student was singled out because of his religion during a religious session led by an external visitor at a prominent secondary school. Responding to questions from fijivillage News, Commission Director Loukinikini Lewaravu says the reports and video footage ci
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has called on the Ministry of Education to promptly investigate allegations that a student was singled out because of his religion during a religious session led by an external visitor at a prominent secondary school.

Responding to questions from fijivillage News, Commission Director Loukinikini Lewaravu says the reports and video footage circulating on social media raised serious human rights and child protection concerns, although the facts of the matter are still being established.

Lewaravu says any situation in which a child feels targeted, pressured, humiliated or excluded because of their religion, beliefs, identity or background warrants careful examination.

She says every child has the right to learn in a safe, respectful and inclusive environment, adding that schools should be places where all students feel valued regardless of their religion, ethnicity, background or beliefs.

Lewaravu stresses that no child should experience discrimination, intimidation, coercion or treatment that undermines their wellbeing, confidence, sense of belonging or educational experience.

She says child safeguarding extends beyond protecting children from physical harm and also includes protection from emotional and psychological harm, discrimination, bullying, intimidation, inappropriate behaviour, harmful practices and any form of pressure relating to personal beliefs, religion, culture or identity.

The Commission is reminding schools and other duty bearers that Section 22 of the 2013 Constitution guarantees freedom of religion, conscience and belief, and provides that a child attending an educational institution cannot be required to receive religious instruction or participate in religious observances relating to a religion that is not their own without the consent of a parent or lawful guardian.

Lewaravu also highlighted Section 41 of the Constitution, which states that the best interests of the child must be the primary consideration in every matter concerning the child, including decisions and activities within the school environment .

The Commission is recommending that the Ministry of Education review policies governing external visitors and speakers to ensure appropriate vetting, supervision and child safeguarding standards are consistently applied across all schools.

They have also recommended that schools strengthen child safeguarding policies, establish clear codes of conduct for external visitors, including religious leaders, provide regular training on child rights and religious diversity, and ensure children and parents have safe and accessible avenues to raise concerns.

Lewaravu says Fiji's cultural and religious diversity is one of the country's greatest strengths, and schools play a vital role in fostering mutual respect, understanding and social cohesion.

The Commission says it stands ready to work with the Ministry of Education and other stakeholders to strengthen child safeguarding measures and ensure every child can learn in an environment that protects their rights, dignity and wellbeing.

Meanwhile, the Commission's statement follows public concern over a video circulating on social media that appears to show Christian preacher and content creator Osaslugo addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka during school hours.

In the footage, Osaslugo appears to single out a student identified as Muslim and tells him that because he does not follow Jesus Christ or Christianity, he will not go to heaven.

The video has drawn widespread criticism online, with many questioning whether the child was publicly singled out because of his religion and whether the school had approved the session.

item.title: Singh returns to Fiji for reunion with Tavarau Primary school classmates after 40 years
item.description: After more than two decades of living in the United States, Charanjit Singh has returned to Fiji for a special occasion – reuniting with his primary school classmates after 40 years. Originally from Ba and now living in Sacramento, California, Singh arrived in Fiji yesterday and says the reunion with his former classmates from Tavarau Primary School is something he has been looking forward to.
item.content:encoded:
After more than two decades of living in the United States, Charanjit Singh has returned to Fiji for a special occasion – reuniting with his primary school classmates after 40 years.

Originally from Ba and now living in Sacramento, California, Singh arrived in Fiji yesterday and says the reunion with his former classmates from Tavarau Primary School is something he has been looking forward to.

He says he and his classmates, who studied together from Year One to Eight, will gather in Lautoka next week for their primary school reunion, adding that meeting his old friends again after four decades is a special moment.

Reflecting on his childhood in Ba, Singh remembers the simple days of going to school barefoot, later wearing KD shoes, and making school bags out of plastic bags.

He says those experiences created many unforgettable memories and strong friendships that have lasted over the years.

Insert: HINDI Singh on his trip 23rd July.

Singh moved to Sacramento in December 1999, where he now owns a banquet hall and a Fijian restaurant, which he says was inspired by the demand for authentic Fijian cuisine.

He says the restaurant was established after people attending events at his banquet hall praised the food and encouraged him to share Fijian cuisine with the wider community.

Singh says the restaurant has been operating since 2016 and showcases Fiji's culture and flavours in California.

He says returning to Fiji has brought back many fond memories, and he is looking forward to reconnecting with friends and celebrating their shared journey.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: 'No consent was given' - Parent speaks out over viral school preaching video
item.description: A parent whose child was present during the school session shown in a viral social media video says families were not informed or asked for consent before a preacher addressed students, and is now seeking answers from the school's administration. The parent, who requested anonymity, told fijivillage News that they only became aware of the incident after the video began circulating on Facebook yest
item.content:encoded:
A parent whose child was present during the school session shown in a viral social media video says families were not informed or asked for consent before a preacher addressed students, and is now seeking answers from the school's administration.

The parent, who requested anonymity, told fijivillage News that they only became aware of the incident after the video began circulating on Facebook yesterday.

The video, which has attracted widespread attention online , appears to show Christian preacher and content creator Osaslugo addressing students at a prominent school in Lautoka before singling out a student he identifies as Muslim.

He appears to tell the student that because he does not follow Jesus Christ or Christianity, he will not go to heaven.

Many are questioning whether it was appropriate for a student to be publicly singled out because of his religion in a school environment.

The parent adds that they are still not happy after seeing the video and are trying to arrange a meeting with the school principal to establish how the session was allowed to take place.

The parent further states that neither they nor, to their knowledge, other parents had been notified in advance that a preacher would be addressing students.

The incident has raised broader questions about whether appropriate approval processes were followed, whether parents should have been informed beforehand, and how religious activities are managed in schools.

We are still awaiting a response from the Ministry of Education regarding the incident, including whether permission was granted for the session, what safeguards were in place, and what action, if any, will be taken.

We have also sent questions to the Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission.

Fiji is a secular state, and the Constitution guarantees freedom of religion and protection from discrimination on religious grounds.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Women urged to lead the fight against child abuse
item.description: Women are being encouraged to play a greater role in protecting children from abuse while also taking advantage of government programmes aimed at improving their economic wellbeing. Speaking at the Lautoka Women's Forum at Lautoka Mall, Minister for Women, Children and Social Protection Sashi Kiran says the Ministry continues to work closely with women, children and older persons, with a strong fo
item.content:encoded:
Women are being encouraged to play a greater role in protecting children from abuse while also taking advantage of government programmes aimed at improving their economic wellbeing.

Speaking at the Lautoka Women's Forum at Lautoka Mall, Minister for Women, Children and Social Protection Sashi Kiran says the Ministry continues to work closely with women, children and older persons, with a strong focus on addressing emerging social issues, including gender-based violence and child protection.

Kiran says one of the Ministry's key priorities is addressing gender-based violence, and through the Women's Forums, awareness is being raised on serious issues affecting families, including child sexual abuse, child sexual exploitation and the dangers of children and young people sharing intimate images online.

She says parents and communities need to have these conversations, understand the risks and work together to keep children safe.

The Minister also encourages women to make use of economic opportunities available through Government and its partners, highlighting the Ministry's National Action Plan on Women's Economic Empowerment.

K iran says many women are looking for opportunities to improve their livelihoods, whether through product development training, grants or access to markets, and the Government is creating more pathways for women to succeed.

She also stresses that support is available across different government ministries through programmes that help women build skills, start businesses, access finance and improve household incomes.

During the forum, women raised concerns about accessing markets for their products and sought information on skills training, grants and other support available for small businesses.

Participants also asked about the Ministry's social protection schemes, services for vulnerable women and children, and adoption processes.

The forum also featured a parenting awareness session by UNICEF Fiji, where Social Policy Specialist Akuila Sovanivalu and Halitesh Datt introduced the Bebbo Parenting App and assisted mothers in downloading the free application.

The app provides guidance on child nutrition, health, early learning, developmental milestones and positive parenting for children from birth to six years of age .

The Lautoka Women's Forum brought together women's groups from communities across Lautoka to discuss issues affecting women, children and families, while learning more about government initiatives and services available to support them.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Insufficient evidence led to charges being dropped against men accused over Laqere checkpoint incident
item.description: The Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions says charges against Domoniko Tabao and Jone Rakatia over the alleged attempted murder of a female police officer at the Laqere checkpoint on the 26th of April this year were discontinued due to insufficient evidence. The ODPP confirms to fijivillage News that after careful consideration of the evidence, legal analysis and the law, the charges agai
item.content:encoded:
The Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions says charges against Domoniko Tabao and Jone Rakatia over the alleged attempted murder of a female police officer at the Laqere checkpoint on the 26th of April this year were discontinued due to insufficient evidence.

The ODPP confirms to fijivillage News that after careful consideration of the evidence, legal analysis and the law, the charges against the two men were discontinued by way of nolle prosequi because there was insufficient evidence to proceed.

The ODPP says this does not prevent the State from re-laying charges against the two men should new evidence emerge.

Meanwhile, the charges against co-accused Saimoni Rokotunidau will continue.

Rokotunidau is charged with one count of attempted murder, one count of unlawful possession of illicit drugs, one count of going equipped, one count of serious assault, and one count of refusing to have his fingerprints and photographs taken.

item.title: Ju Ben excited to perform new music at CFL's 41st anniversary celebration this Saturday
item.description: Local rapper Peni Roqara, famously known as Ju Ben, is set to debut two new tracks when he takes to the stage at the CFL Gratitude Concert this Saturday, as Communications Fiji Limited celebrates its 41st anniversary. Speaking to fijivillage News, Ju Ben says he is looking forward to sharing his new music with fans and meeting the people who have supported his journey over the years.
item.content:encoded:
Local rapper Peni Roqara, famously known as Ju Ben, is set to debut two new tracks when he takes to the stage at the CFL Gratitude Concert this Saturday, as Communications Fiji Limited celebrates its 41st anniversary.

Speaking to fijivillage News, Ju Ben says he is looking forward to sharing his new music with fans and meeting the people who have supported his journey over the years.

The rapper says he is especially excited to perform for children, many of whom regularly sing along to his songs during live performances.

Ju Ben says he feels privileged to create positive music for the younger generation and hopes to use his platform to educate and motivate them.

He says his fans are the reason he continues to make music, adding that he creates songs for listeners who can relate to his experiences.

He also reflected on the role Communications Fiji Limited has played in his music career, saying the company was one of the first major broadcasters to support his music during his early days.

Insert: Ju Ben - Looking forward to the concert July 22

He is encouraging families to attend the free event at Albert Park in Suva, where he plans to perform several fan favourites, including his latest releases 'V ainaki' and ' Now You Know'

The CFL Gratitude Concert begins at 1pm on Saturday at Albert Park, with live entertainment, food stalls and family activities throughout the afternoon as the company celebrates 41 years of serving Fijians.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Clarke questions Mason on whether she disclosed she had issues in NZ when taking up COI job
